author: @Trivimania
text: I find it nice to see that you create all kinds of generators, but its a pitty that we never see ANY info about volts AND amps together. Agreed, it will be probably more milliAmps and you clearly state that you are just looking for smaller scale proof of concepts and not the optimal production, but could you somehow share a little info ? Like for example the smaller turbines you create using the magnetic levitating bearing. You test and show voltages, but wouldn't it be nice to also for a change show milliamps and so a little bit of basis calculus? I do understand that these small turbines will be producing very little, but what is very Little? Do they even achieve for example 1Watt of output ? Could you please give a little more info about that? Thank you very much

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
Does this air inlet have a 'whistle pinch' or any other method of focusing, deflesting or directing the air to increase optimal velocity and impingement?

Also, while it may not matter at all, especially if you are only breathing into it, a slightly larger, and more side oriented exhaust would ever so slightly help the reduction of back pressure. ~( 'w')/

Again, I do realize, that when one is talking this tiny little mechanism, at such low power inputs, it is really hard to notice much of a gain, but, for those wishing to experiment further into this, seeing as they often use 'accelerator needles' on such devices as Pelton wheels, and from this gain much more conversion of the energy potential of the input fluid, it only makes sense to do the same here.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes it does mate - if you have a look at the tinkercad drawing you will see it but I am doing a follow up to this and I will highlight the pinch in that video

author: @drsatan3231
text: By sealing the fan in with the cap you've increased the resistance the blades are exposed to. That's why it's stopping so quick

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no that's not why mate - the generator has a lump of steel and magnets in it and the magnets are trying to rotate past the steel - they can't - that's why

replies.author: @drsatan3231
replies.text:
​@ThinkingandTinkering i understand theres magnetic resistance but there doesnt appear to be an exhaust for the air which passes the fan. 

It'll just spin around the fan and once the momentum runs out it'll escape back the way it came. The air you blow into it just fights itself 

Do a quick test with cap on vs cap off

author: @Keeper-of-Knowledge369
text:
To build the Yildiz Magnet Motor with an output of 20 kW, you will need the following materials:

Rotor: You will need a circular metal plate with a diameter of 400 mm, a thickness of 50 mm, and a weight of approximately 16 kg. You will also need 12 
cylindrical neodymium magnets with dimensions of 50 mm x 20 mm x 10 mm and a weight of approximately 60 g each.

Stator: You will need a circular metal plate with a diameter of 420 mm and a thickness of 30 mm, with 12 slots evenly spaced around the circumference. Each 
slot should be able to hold a stator coil. You will also need 12 stator coils made of copper wire with a gauge of 14 AWG and a length of 100 meters each.

Bearing: You will need two ball bearings with a diameter of 50 mm and a thickness of 20 mm, one for the top and one for the bottom of the motor.

Frame: You will need a sturdy frame to hold the rotor and stator in place. The frame should be made of metal and should be able to support the weight of the 
rotor and stator.

Wiring: You will need wiring to connect the stator coils in a series and to connect the motor to an external power source.

Instructions:

Begin by constructing the rotor. Attach the 12 neodymium magnets to the circular metal plate, evenly spaced around the circumference. Use a strong adhesive 
to hold the magnets in place.

Construct the stator by winding the copper wire around a cylindrical object with a diameter of 20 mm, such as a pencil, to create 12 coils. Each coil should be 
approximately 100 meters in length.

Attach the stator to the circular metal plate of the stator, with the coils in the slots, using a strong adhesive.

Attach the ball bearings to the top and bottom of the motor frame. Ensure that they are securely fixed in place.

Insert the rotor into the stator, making sure that the magnets are aligned with the stator coils.

Connect the stator coils in a series by soldering the ends of the wires together.

Connect the motor to an external power source, such as a battery or power supply, using the wiring. The input voltage should be 220 V, and the input current 
should be 91 A.

Start the motor using the external power source.

Note: It is important to ensure that the motor is properly balanced and that the bearings are well-lubricated to prevent excessive wear and tear. It is also 
important to monitor the motor's temperature and ensure that it does not overheat.

FOR MORE INFORMATION, leave me a private message. >>This message was only intended for Robert-Murray Smith<<

replies.author: @Keeper-of-Knowledge369
replies.text:
Materials needed:

1 Rotor with 12 evenly spaced magnet holders
12 Magnets (50mm x 20mm x 10mm)
1 Stator with 12 evenly spaced coils
12 Coils made of 14 AWG copper wire
1 Shaft to hold the rotor
2 Bearings to support the shaft
1 Base to mount the motor
Screws and bolts for assembly

replies.author: @undernetjack
replies.text:
Not to discourage, but I see two issues with that. One, personally, I try to limit water use, and the taps are on only minutes a day. Two, the water provider has to pay to pressurize the water to get it to consumers, they would not be ok with adding an energy capture device.

I do like the idea, just perhaps on a river, or as a protest against water/energy inflation. If enough people did it, nah they'd just raise rates for others, it'd have to be a boycott across the board.
Anyway, cheers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's more than not ok mate -it is actually illegal to do that

author: @jamest.5001
text: How about making a funnel like thing,  that has a ring that can hold a trash bag full of air, like a 40-55 gallon lung to blow the torch! Then squeeze the bag,. Or if you really want to make something, make one of the accordion like blower used to start fires in a fireplace, or in a forge, it could be two sheets of builders board, 6" x 12" with a a handle on end, a tee with check valves on one plate with a hinge connecting them at the opposite from handle end, with the tee connected near the hinge,  use a semi thick paper, not construction paper, but  paper bad would work, cover with clear packing tape on both sides,  fold back and forth zigzag, abd use duct tape  ti connect to the paddles,  sealing all sides, allow the paddles to open to about 60° it should move a decent amount of air, with a 12mm I.d. tee to aboyt 20 mm tee, with chevk valves, possibly using flaps, or balls yo firm a valve, making a intake and exhaust,  tgr exhaust using a bit of hose  going to the torch, if the pavking tape isn't wirking out, duct tape should work well!
